# Keel — full documentation for retrieval

> The SQLite of durable execution: production-grade resilience and opt-in durable
> execution as an in-process library, zero code changes, policy in one
> `keel.toml`. This file is the self-contained corpus — every feature with one
> canonical example, plus the CLI and error-code reference. Stable headings; an
> agent can retrieve any section without following a link.

## What Keel is

Keel wraps a program's outbound effects at the call boundary and applies a
policy: retries, exponential backoff with jitter, per-attempt timeouts, circuit
breakers, rate limits, response caching, and — opt-in — crash-resumable durable
flows. It runs inside your process (no daemon, no port, no login); state is files
under `.keel/`. Add it with `keel run`; remove the package and your code runs
identically (minus resilience). Two tiers:

- **Tier 1 (resilience):** always on, per intercepted call. cache → rate →
  breaker → timeout → retry.
- **Tier 2 (durability):** opt-in per flow. Journals each effect; a crashed run
  re-executes from the top and substitutes already-completed effects from the
  journal instead of re-firing them.

## The "just works" ladder

### Level 0 — zero config

```
keel run app.py            # python
keel run app.mjs           # node
```

Every discovered outbound call gets conservative production defaults (30s
timeout, 3 retries on transient errors — conn/timeout/429/5xx — with jittered
exponential backoff, per-host breaker). LLM provider calls get the LLM pack. One
line of output, then silence. Hard rules: never change success-path semantics;
never retry a non-idempotent call (POST without an idempotency key) by default —
it is marked observed-not-retried (KEEL-E014); if Keel can't wrap something
safely it does nothing and says so in `keel doctor`.

### Level 1 — `keel init`

```
keel init                  # writes keel.toml from static scan + observed runs
keel init --diff           # preview adds/removes against an existing keel.toml
```

Generated entries cite `file:line` evidence and observed traffic; deleting any
entry falls back to the same built-in defaults. The generated file is the
documentation.

### Level 2 — durable flows (config-only)

```toml
# keel.toml
[flows]
entrypoints = ["py:pipeline:main"]     # module `pipeline`, function `main`
```

```
keel run pipeline.py       # runs it as a durable flow
# kill -9 it mid-run, then re-run: resumes, replaying completed steps
```

Completed steps are substituted from `.keel/journal.db` (their effects never
re-fire); the rest run live. `keel flows` lists resumable/completed flows;
`keel trace <flow>` shows the step ledger. Time (`time.time`/`time_ns`) and
`random.random` reads inside a flow are virtualized so a resumed run sees the
same values.

v0.1 limits (precise, never silent): a flow needs a journal (native core).
Async flow bodies are supported: an `async def` entrypoint's awaited
intercepted calls route through the same open flow handle as a synchronous
flow's, journaled identically; concurrent awaited effects (`asyncio.gather`)
are admitted — and journaled — in the order their calls reach the handle,
never in completion order.

### Level 3 — fleet

One line (`journal = "postgres://…"`) moves the journal to Postgres for
multi-process, cross-host fleet recovery; nothing else changes. Lease
arbitration reads the DATABASE's clock, not any one process's, so it stays
correct under host clock drift.

## Targets

A policy entry is `[target."<key>"]`. Kinds:

- **host** — e.g. `[target."api.stripe.com"]`, matched by request host.
- **`py:` / `ts:` functions** — e.g. `[target."py:pipeline.do_step"]`; listing a
  function asserts it is safe to retry (idempotent).
- **semantic** — `llm:<provider>` (openai/anthropic/google-genai/…),
  `tool:<name>`, `mcp:<server>`. Auto-detected from provider hosts / framework
  adapters.

Canonical policy example:

```toml
[target."api.stripe.com"]              # seen in: billing.py:41
retry       = { attempts = 5, schedule = "exp(200ms, x2, max 30s, jitter)" }
idempotency = { header = "Idempotency-Key" }

[target."llm:openai"]                   # seen in: agent.py:12
retry = { attempts = 6, on = ["429","5xx","timeout"] }
rate  = "60/min"
cache = { mode = "dev" }                # dev-loop cache; inert when KEEL_ENV=prod
```

## Resilience features (one example each)

- **Retry + backoff:** `retry = { attempts = 5, schedule = "exp(200ms, x2, max 30s, jitter)", on = ["conn","timeout","429","5xx"] }`. 429 backoff honors `Retry-After` when larger than the scheduled wait.
- **Timeout:** `timeout = "30s"` — enforced per attempt (KEEL-E011 on breach).
- **Circuit breaker:** `breaker = { failures = 5, cooldown = "15s" }` — opens after N failures, half-opens after cooldown (KEEL-E012 while open).
- **Rate limit:** `rate = "60/min"` — token bucket; KEEL-E013 if the wait budget is exceeded.
- **Idempotency:** `idempotency = { header = "Idempotency-Key" }` — makes a non-idempotent call retryable (otherwise KEEL-E014, observed-not-retried).
- **Cache / LLM dev-cache:** `cache = { mode = "dev" }` — identical request+args replay off `KEEL_ENV=prod`; with the native core + journal it persists across separate runs (a repeated prompt costs ~0).

## LLM / agent frameworks

`llm:<provider>` calls get a default pack (retry on 429/5xx/timeout with
provider-aware backoff, rate limiting, dev cache). Framework adapter packs are
auto-detected from dependencies (AI SDK middleware, `mcp:` transports, etc.) and
wrap tool/LLM calls with zero agent-code changes. See dx-spec §4.

## CLI reference

Global: `--json` on any command emits deterministic JSON (sorted keys, no
timestamps) instead of prose. Same command, two audiences.

- `keel run <target> [args...]` — run a script/package/dir under Keel. `--disable` runs it byte-identically with Keel off.
- `keel init [--diff] [--stamp] [--agents]` — generate `keel.toml` from evidence; `--diff` previews; `--stamp` dates the header; `--agents` writes the Keel section into `AGENTS.md` instead.
- `keel doctor [--effective-policy]` — the honesty report: what's wrapped, what's visible-but-unwrapped and why, adapter version pins, policy validity, journal backend. `--effective-policy` prints the composed `defaults < packs < user` policy `keel_configure` actually receives.
- `keel status` — coverage %, retries saved this week, breaker events, cache hit rate, resumable flows.
- `keel explain <KEEL-E0NN>` — the exact what/why/next for an error code.
- `keel flows [--dead]` — list durable flows (id, entrypoint, status, steps, age).
- `keel flows suggest` — analyze candidate flow entrypoints for replay-safety (effect counts, idempotent-unsafe effects, time/random reads, an estimated replay-safe verdict).
- `keel flows add <entrypoint> [--diff]` — designate an entrypoint as a durable flow: appends it to `[flows] entrypoints`, idempotent re-run.
- `keel flows resume [<flow> | --all]` — re-invoke a resumable flow's recorded entrypoint through `keel run`.
- `keel trace <flow>` — one flow's steps (outcomes, attempts, timings).
- `keel replay <flow> [--step N]` — a journal-driven dry run of what re-entering the flow would do: which steps substitute, which re-execute, where replay resumes.
- `keel tail [--run ID] [--no-follow]` — live view of attempts/backoffs/breaker transitions while a program runs, reading `.keel/events/`; no daemon.
- `keel fsck [--fix] [--prune AGE]` — journal integrity check (orphan steps, dangling leases, expired cache), safe repairs, and retention pruning.
- `keel mcp` — serve this project over MCP on stdio (JSON-RPC 2.0; no daemon, exits on EOF). Six tools: `get_status`, `get_doctor_report`, `propose_policy` (returns a keel.toml diff), `get_trace`, `list_flows`, `explain_error`. Each tool's text result is byte-identical to the matching command's `--json` output.
- `keel record run <target> [args...]` / `keel record list` / `keel record test <recording>` — capture every intercepted effect during a run into `.keel/recordings/`, then turn a recording into a replayable offline test fixture (a pytest fixture or `node:test` file) that fails loudly on any unmatched effect.
- `keel sim <plan>` — replay a declarative fault plan (injected errors, latency, crash-restart points) against a script or a recording, asserting the policy holds (retries within budget, breaker opens as configured, flows resume). Adapter-level fault injection, not hermetic simulation.

## Dependency activation (without `keel run`)

When another tool owns the process (test runners, `agents-cli`, `adk api_server`, uvicorn), activate Keel via environment variables:

- **Python**: the `keelrun` wheel ships a `.pth` shim gated on `KEEL_ENABLE=1`. Set it in your `.env` and every Python process boots with the same policy engine as `keel run` — loading `keel.toml` from the working directory, or from `KEEL_CWD=<dir>` when config lives in the deployable app. `KEEL_DISABLE=1` always wins.
- **Node**: add `NODE_OPTIONS="--import keelrun/register"` alongside `KEEL_ENABLE=1`. Same gate and cwd-search semantics as Python.
- Activation is fail-open: a broken install or invalid `keel.toml` prints one `keel ▸` warning line; your app runs unwrapped. What stays a `keel run` feature: the preflight resilience advisory, `keel record` / `keel sim` wiring, and flow-entrypoint dispatch (designated via `[flows] entrypoints`, not auto-detected).

## Error codes (frozen taxonomy)

Every user-facing error carries a `KEEL-E0NN` code, a first line (what/why/next),
a `--json` twin, and a trace reference. `keel explain <code>` prints the detail.

- KEEL-E001 policy-invalid — keel.toml failed validation (wrong type / unknown key / bad literal). Next: `keel doctor`; validate against policy.schema.json.
- KEEL-E002 policy-target-unknown — strict mode only; unknown target with no entry.
- KEEL-E003 envelope-decode — a request/attempt-result envelope could not be decoded at the FFI boundary (version mismatch / corruption).
- KEEL-E004 envelope-version-unsupported — language package and core binary are incompatible releases.
- KEEL-E005 unsupported-configuration — the policy is valid but asks for a capability this build/configuration cannot provide: a `[flows]` designation with no journal / no native backend, or `KEEL_BACKEND=native` with no native module. (Async effects inside a flow are supported — serialized in await order, per conformance/README.md's Tier 2 ordering rule — and no longer trigger this code.) Next: build the native module and make `.keel/` writable; `keel doctor`.
- KEEL-E010 attempts-exhausted — failed on the final permitted attempt; original error re-raised unchanged.
- KEEL-E011 timeout — exceeded the policy timeout.
- KEEL-E012 breaker-open — the breaker is open; the call failed fast, unattempted.
- KEEL-E013 rate-budget-exceeded — could not queue under the rate limit within the wait budget.
- KEEL-E014 non-idempotent-not-retried — a retryable failure on a non-idempotent call; observed, not retried (Level 0 hard rule). Add `idempotency = { header = "…" }` to make it retryable.
- KEEL-E015 non-retryable-error — error class not in `retry.on`; original re-raised.
- KEEL-E020 cache-codec — a response could not be (de)serialized for the cache.
- KEEL-E030 flow-lease-held — the flow is being executed by another process (live lease).
- KEEL-E031 flow-nondeterminism — on replay the code diverged from the journal (or code changed). Set `flows.on_nondeterminism = "warn"|"branch"` to continue.
- KEEL-E032 flow-dead — the flow exhausted its resume-attempt cap (poison flow).
- KEEL-E040 internal — a Keel bug (invariant violation inside the core). File an issue with the trace id.

## DX invariants (tested in CI as behaviors)

1. Zero code changes in Python/TS (one optional attribute in Rust).
2. Uninstall = remove the package; code runs identically.
3. No daemon, no port, no login — state is files in `.keel/`.
4. Silence on success, precision on failure (what/why/next/trace).
5. Never swallow errors — the original propagates unchanged, with a `keel` attachment.
6. Everything has `--json` and stable exit codes.
7. Works offline — no telemetry, no phone-home.
8. Fast — `keel run` adds <100 ms startup; wrapping overhead ≤10 µs/call (measured ~0.8 µs, worst case, including the event sink and idempotency injection), published in the README.

## Determinism (for agents)

Identical inputs produce byte-identical `--json` (sorted keys, no wall-clock
timestamps unless asked). Suggestions are emitted as applyable diffs. Run
`keel doctor --json` before changing resilience behavior; `keel init --agents`
seeds `AGENTS.md` so future sessions inherit this context.

## Install

```
pip install keelrun keelrun-cli                # Python: library + CLI
npm install keelrun keelrun-cli                # Node: library + CLI
cargo add keelrun --rename keel                # Rust: library (#[keel::wrap])
cargo install keelrun-cli                      # Rust: CLI (cargo has no single command for both)
uvx --from keelrun-cli keel run app.py         # zero-install CLI, any language
```

Building from source (contributors):

```
maturin develop -m crates/keel-py/Cargo.toml   # native keel_core into the venv (Python)
pip install -e 'python/keel[dev]'
node node/keel/bin/keel-node-run.mjs app.mjs   # Node from-source `keel run`
cargo build -p keelrun-cli                      # the `keel` binary
```

## Conformance

34 scenarios in `conformance/scenarios/` (21 Tier 1: 01–15 plus
18/19/20/21–22/23; 13 Tier 2: 16–27, with two Tier-2 scenarios sharing number
24), run by the real Rust core (all of them — Tier 2 additionally against a
real Postgres journal backend), the Rust/Python/Node stubs (every Tier 1
scenario; Tier 2 skipped by design — no journal), and the native front ends.
`conformance/README.md` is the normative semantics.
